turns.speaker: host
turns.text: Welcome to the show! Today we're diving into the Transformer attention mechanism — the breakthrough that revolutionized AI. Can you walk us through what made this architecture so different from everything that came before?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Before Transformers, models like LSTMs read sentences like a human reading word-by-word, carrying forward a fading memory. The Transformer instead looks at all words simultaneously through self-attention—like being able to scan an entire page at once, instantly grasping how every word relates to every other word.

turns.speaker: host
turns.text: That's a beautiful analogy! So how does this "scanning" actually work under the hood? What are these queries, keys, and values I keep hearing about?

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: Think of a library search: your query is what you're looking for, book titles are keys describing what's available, and the book contents are the values. Each word projects itself into all three roles—seeking information, advertising what it offers, and holding content to share.

turns.speaker: expert
turns.text: It gets even better—when a query matches a key, they compute a dot product similarity score. But with high dimensions, those scores explode, pushing the softmax into tiny gradients. Dividing by the square root of the dimension is like adjusting the volume so nothing distorts.
